text: The surgeon encounters a challenging case during laparoscopic cholecystectomy, with the liver visibly adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, limiting the ability to retract the gallbladder freely towards the right shoulder.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree telescope to visualize the calyx anatomy, which appears relatively straightforward. The rubial sulcus is identified, and dissection is performed above this sulcus. The peritoneal reflection over the calyx region resembles an envelope, which is carefully peeled off during the procedure.

The surgeon pulls the gallbladder laterally and caudally to enhance visibility of the medial peritoneal reflection. After addressing the medial reflection, the gallbladder is pulled medially to peel off the lateral peritoneal reflection, with visible traction applied to the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses left-hand forceps to dissect the peritoneum laterally, ensuring to stay above the rubial sulcus. Approximately one-third of the peritoneal reflection over the gallbladder is dissected before proceeding to the Calot's region. Small bands or peritoneal reflections around the cystic duct are managed with a hook instrument.

The surgeon creates a window between the cystic duct and cystic artery, identifying the cystic artery for skeletonization. Blunt dissection with a Maryland dissector is an option at this stage, though the surgeon's preference dictates the technique used.

The surgeon confirms the cystic artery is clear and skeletonized, then proceeds to dissect the gallbladder body from the liver bed to clear the Calot's triangle. The triangle is bounded laterally by the cystic duct, medially by the liver's undersurface, and inferiorly by the common hepatic duct, containing the cystic artery and a lymph node.

The surgeon applies three clips on the cystic duct: one distal near the neck of the gallbladder and two proximal, ensuring a minimum distance of two millimeters between the proximal clips. A clip is also placed on the cystic artery. The cystic duct is then cut between the clips using scissors, and the cystic artery is coagulated with a hook. The gallbladder is separated from the liver bed using electrocautery.
The surgeon uses an L hook to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed, employing techniques such as hooking the tissue and cooking it or using the heel of the hook to find the dissection plane.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to peel the gallbladder from the liver bed, minimizing oozing compared to using gauze. As the dissection progresses towards the fundus of the gallbladder, the forceps are readjusted to hold the gallbladder near its body, facilitating dissection in the fundus region.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree telescope to obtain a clear view of the liver adherent to the anterior abdominal wall. Electrocautery hook and blunt dissection with Maryland forceps are employed to safely dissect above the Rouvière's sulcus.
